title: REQ-DFM-03: Assembly Documentation Package
description:
Create complete assembly documentation for manufacturing.

## Documentation Package Contents

### 1. Assembly Drawing (PDF)
**Purpose:** Visual reference for assembly technicians

**Content:**
- Board outline with dimensions
- Component placement (top and bottom)
- Reference designators visible
- Mounting hole locations
- Connector orientations
- Polarity markings highlighted
- DNP (Do Not Place) components identified

**Format:** PDF, A3 or A4 size, scaled to fit

### 2. Bill of Materials (BOM)
**Format:** CSV or Excel

**Required Columns:**
| Column | Description | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| Item | Line number | 1 |
| Qty | Quantity per board | 2 |
| Reference | Component designators | R1, R2 |
| Value | Component value | 10kΩ |
| Package | Footprint | 0603 |
| Description | Full description | Resistor, 10k, 1%, 0603 |
| Manufacturer | Preferred mfg | Yageo |
| MPN | Manufacturer part number | RC0603FR-0710KL |
| Supplier | Distributor | Digi-Key |
| Supplier PN | Distributor part number | 311-10.0KHRCT-ND |
| DNP | Do Not Place flag | No |
| Notes | Special instructions | 1% tolerance required |

### 3. Pick-and-Place File (CPL)
**Format:** CSV

**Required Columns:**
| Column | Description | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| Designator | Reference | R1 |
| Mid X | X coordinate (mm) | 25.400 |
| Mid Y | Y coordinate (mm) | 38.100 |
| Layer | Top/Bottom | Top |
| Rotation | Degrees | 90 |

**Notes:**
- Coordinates relative to board origin (bottom-left)
- Rotation per manufacturer convention
- Verify coordinate system with fab house

### 4. Gerber Files (Fabrication)
**Layer Set:**
| File | Extension | Content |
|------|-----------|---------|
| Top Copper | .GTL | L1 artwork |
| Ground Plane | .G2L | L2 artwork |
| Power Plane | .G3L | L3 artwork |
| Bottom Copper | .GBL | L4 artwork |
| Top Solder Mask | .GTS | Top mask |
| Bottom Solder Mask | .GBS | Bottom mask |
| Top Silkscreen | .GTO | Top legend |
| Bottom Silkscreen | .GBO | Bottom legend |
| Top Paste | .GTP | Stencil apertures |
| Bottom Paste | .GBP | Stencil apertures |
| Board Outline | .GKO | Mechanical |
| Drill | .DRL | Excellon format |

### 5. Drill File
**Format:** Excellon (NC drill)

**Content:**
- All through-hole locations
- Via locations (if plated separate)
- Tool table with drill sizes

### 6. IPC-D-356 Netlist (Optional)
**Purpose:** Electrical test verification
**Format:** IPC-D-356A
**Content:** Net connectivity for ICT

### 7. 3D Model (STEP)
**Purpose:** Mechanical interference check
**Format:** STEP AP214

### 8. Assembly Instructions (THT)
**Purpose:** Guide for hand-soldered components

**Components for Hand Assembly:**
- Q1, Q2 (IGBTs) - TO-247
- C_BUS1, C_BUS2 - Radial capacitors
- D1, D2 - TO-220 diodes
- K_BYPASS - Relay
- L_BUCK - Through-hole inductor
- All connectors

**Instructions:**
1. Verify PCB passed SMT inspection
2. Install components in height order (shortest first)
3. Apply flux to leads
4. Solder with appropriate iron temperature (350-380°C)
5. Inspect joints for proper wetting
6. Clean flux residue if required

## Quality Checkpoints

### SMT Assembly Checklist
- [ ] Solder paste print inspection (SPI)
- [ ] Component placement verification
- [ ] Reflow profile recorded
- [ ] AOI (Automated Optical Inspection)
- [ ] Touch-up any defects

### THT Assembly Checklist
- [ ] Component identification verified
- [ ] Polarity correct (caps, diodes)
- [ ] IGBT mounting torque verified
- [ ] Solder joint visual inspection
- [ ] Heatsink assembly (if applicable)

### Final Assembly Checklist
- [ ] All connectors seated properly
- [ ] No solder bridges (magnified inspection)
- [ ] No lifted pads or traces
- [ ] Board clean (no flux residue)
- [ ] Ready for functional test

## Deliverables
- [ ] Assembly drawing (PDF)
- [ ] BOM (CSV/Excel)
- [ ] Pick-and-place file (CSV)
- [ ] Gerber package (ZIP)
- [ ] Drill file (Excellon)
- [ ] STEP model
- [ ] THT assembly instructions
- [ ] Quality checklists

## Acceptance Criteria
- All documentation complete and consistent
- BOM matches schematic component count
- Pick-and-place coordinates verified
- Gerber files pass DFM check at fab house
- 3D model shows no interferences
close_reason: Created TDD tests: validators/documentation.py with BOM completeness, CPL coordinates, Gerber layers, and DNP consistency validation

title: Design Review Process
description:
Sub-epic covering design review checklists and sign-off criteria.

## Scope
- Schematic review checklist
- Layout review checklist
- DRC/ERC verification process
- Pre-fabrication sign-off criteria
- Design freeze process

## Review Stages
1. Schematic Review (before layout)
2. Placement Review (component placement complete)
3. Routing Review (routing complete)
4. Final Review (before fab release)

## Participants
- Design engineer (primary)
- Peer review (secondary)
- AI agent verification (automated checks)

## Reference Documents
- IPC-2221B design standard
- IPC-7351B land pattern standard
- KiCad DRC/ERC rules

## Success Criteria
- All checklists completed with sign-off
- DRC zero errors, ERC zero errors
- All review comments resolved
- Fab files verified with Gerber viewer

title: REQ-REV-01: Schematic Review Checklist
description:
Schematic review checklist to be completed before layout begins.

## Pre-Layout Schematic Review

### 1. Power Supply Verification
- [ ] All ICs have correct supply voltage (3.3V vs 5V)
- [ ] Decoupling capacitors on every power pin
- [ ] Bulk capacitors at power entry points
- [ ] Power sequencing correct (if required)
- [ ] Current ratings adequate for all supplies
- [ ] Voltage ratings adequate for all components

### 2. Component Selection
- [ ] All components have valid part numbers
- [ ] Footprints assigned and verified
- [ ] Temperature ratings adequate (125°C for power, 85°C for logic)
- [ ] Voltage ratings include safety margin (>20%)
- [ ] Current ratings include safety margin (>30%)
- [ ] No obsolete or EOL parts

### 3. Net Naming and Hierarchy
- [ ] All nets have meaningful names
- [ ] Power nets consistently named (e.g., +5V, +3V3, GND)
- [ ] No duplicate net names with different meanings
- [ ] Hierarchical sheet connections correct
- [ ] Global labels used appropriately

### 4. Safety Circuit Review
- [ ] OCP circuit values verified
- [ ] OVP circuit values verified
- [ ] Thermal shutdown thresholds correct
- [ ] Gate driver enable/disable logic correct
- [ ] Watchdog timer configured
- [ ] Fault latch operation verified

### 5. Analog Circuit Review
- [ ] ADC input ranges correct (0-3.3V for ESP32)
- [ ] CT burden resistor value verified
- [ ] NTC thermistor divider values correct
- [ ] Filter cutoff frequencies appropriate
- [ ] Op-amp supply rails adequate

### 6. Digital Interface Review
- [ ] SPI signals correct (CLK, MOSI, MISO, CS polarity)
- [ ] I2C pull-up resistors present
- [ ] UART TX/RX correctly assigned
- [ ] GPIO assignments documented
- [ ] PWM outputs to correct pins

### 7. Gate Drive Circuit Review
- [ ] UCC21550 input logic matches ESP32 levels
- [ ] Dead-time requirements documented
- [ ] Bootstrap capacitor value adequate
- [ ] Gate resistor values verified
- [ ] Miller clamp threshold correct

### 8. Power Stage Review
- [ ] IGBT voltage rating adequate (1200V > 340V × 2.5)
- [ ] IGBT current rating adequate (40A > 22A × 1.5)
- [ ] Snubber component values verified
- [ ] Bus capacitor ripple current rating adequate
- [ ] Resonant tank component values per simulation

### 9. Connectivity Verification
- [ ] ERC passes with zero errors
- [ ] No unconnected pins (or explicitly marked NC)
- [ ] No floating inputs
- [ ] All test points connected
- [ ] All connectors have correct pinouts

### 10. Documentation
- [ ] All component values on schematic
- [ ] Critical notes added (e.g., DNP, matched pair)
- [ ] Net classes assigned
- [ ] Power dissipation noted for power components
- [ ] Reference documents linked

## Sign-Off

| Reviewer | Date | Status | Comments |
|----------|------|--------|----------|
| Design Engineer | | [ ] Pass [ ] Fail | |
| Peer Review | | [ ] Pass [ ] Fail | |

## Deliverables
- [ ] Completed checklist with sign-off
- [ ] ERC report (zero errors)
- [ ] Updated schematic if issues found
- [ ] Review meeting notes

## Acceptance Criteria
- All checklist items verified
- ERC passes with zero errors
- All reviewers sign off
- Issues resolved before layout starts
close_reason: Created comprehensive TDD test suite for schematic review checklist. Implemented 27 tests covering all requirements: power supply verification (8 tests), component selection (7 tests), net naming (5 tests), safety circuits (5 tests), and integration tests (2 tests). Validator module with 21 functions created in tests/requirements/validators/schematic.py. All tests pass with NotImplementedError (TDD approach). See tests/requirements/review/test_schematic.py and README.md for details.

title: REQ-REV-02: Layout Review Checklist
description:
Layout review checklist to be completed before fabrication release.

## Layout Review Checklist

### 1. Board Outline and Mechanical
- [ ] Board dimensions correct (100mm × 150mm)
- [ ] Corner radii correct (3mm)
- [ ] Mounting holes in correct locations (5mm from edge)
- [ ] Keep-outs around mounting holes (3mm radius)
- [ ] Board thickness specified (1.6mm)
- [ ] Height constraints respected per zone

### 2. Layer Stack Verification
- [ ] 4-layer stackup correct (Top/GND/PWR/Bot)
- [ ] Copper weights correct (2oz outer, 1oz inner)
- [ ] Prepreg/core thicknesses specified
- [ ] Controlled impedance not required (verified)

### 3. Component Placement
- [ ] All components placed
- [ ] No component overlaps (3D check)
- [ ] Clearance for heatsink mounting
- [ ] IGBTs on board edge for heatsink
- [ ] ESP32 antenna area clear (15mm keep-out)
- [ ] Connectors accessible from board edge
- [ ] Test points accessible

### 4. Critical Loops (per REQ-ELEC-06)
- [ ] DC bus loop area <5 cm²
- [ ] Gate drive loops <2 cm²
- [ ] Buck converter SW loop <1 cm²
- [ ] Bootstrap loop minimized
- [ ] CT burden loop minimized

### 5. Ground Plane Integrity (per REQ-EMC-01)
- [ ] No slots >30mm in ground plane
- [ ] Signal traces over solid ground
- [ ] Star ground point implemented
- [ ] Via stitching at 5mm along boundaries
- [ ] PGND/CGND/ISOGND properly separated

### 6. Power Distribution
- [ ] Power plane islands correctly defined
- [ ] No power island under SW nodes
- [ ] Via stitching on power islands
- [ ] Current capacity adequate for traces

### 7. Clearance and Creepage (per REQ-SAFE-01)
- [ ] HV to LV clearance >6mm
- [ ] HV to LV creepage >8mm
- [ ] Isolation slots routed
- [ ] UCC21550 barrier respected
- [ ] ADUM1250 barrier respected

### 8. Thermal Management (per REQ-THERM)
- [ ] Thermal via arrays under IGBTs
- [ ] Thermal via arrays under LMR51430
- [ ] Copper pours for heat spreading
- [ ] Thermal relief settings correct
- [ ] No thermal isolated pads

### 9. EMC Layout (per REQ-EMC)
- [ ] Bypass caps within 3mm of IC pins
- [ ] Ground vias at cap pads
- [ ] EMI filter component placement correct
- [ ] SW node area minimized
- [ ] No power plane under SW nodes

### 10. Silkscreen and Markings
- [ ] All reference designators visible
- [ ] Safety warnings present
- [ ] Polarity markings correct
- [ ] Pin 1 indicators present
- [ ] Board revision marked
- [ ] No silkscreen over pads

### 11. Manufacturing Readiness
- [ ] Fiducials present (3 global, local near ESP32)
- [ ] Minimum trace/space rules met (6/6 mil)
- [ ] Minimum via specs met (0.3mm drill)
- [ ] Component spacing adequate for pick-and-place
- [ ] Solder paste layer correct

### 12. DRC/ERC Final Check
- [ ] DRC passes with zero errors
- [ ] All unconnected nets intentional
- [ ] All net classes assigned
- [ ] All design rules applied

## Sign-Off

| Reviewer | Date | Status | Comments |
|----------|------|--------|----------|
| Design Engineer | | [ ] Pass [ ] Fail | |
| Peer Review | | [ ] Pass [ ] Fail | |
| DFM Check (Fab) | | [ ] Pass [ ] Fail | |

## Deliverables
- [ ] Completed checklist with sign-off
- [ ] DRC report (zero errors)
- [ ] 3D model screenshot (interference check)
- [ ] Review meeting notes

## Acceptance Criteria
- All checklist items verified
- DRC passes with zero errors
- All reviewers sign off
- Ready for Gerber generation

title: REQ-REV-03: Pre-Fabrication Sign-Off
description:
Final sign-off checklist before releasing design to fabrication.

## Pre-Fabrication Verification

### 1. Design File Verification
- [ ] Final schematic exported (PDF)
- [ ] Final layout exported (PDF)
- [ ] Gerber files generated
- [ ] Drill files generated
- [ ] Pick-and-place file generated
- [ ] BOM finalized

### 2. Gerber File Inspection
- [ ] Opened in Gerber viewer (GerbV, KiCad viewer, etc.)
- [ ] All layers present and aligned
- [ ] Board outline correct
- [ ] Drill file matches pad locations
- [ ] Solder mask openings correct
- [ ] Silkscreen readable and complete
- [ ] No stray geometry or artifacts

### 3. BOM Verification
- [ ] All components have part numbers
- [ ] All components in stock (or lead time acceptable)
- [ ] No DNP discrepancies
- [ ] Quantities correct
- [ ] Alternates documented if needed

### 4. Design Rule Summary
Confirm these are set correctly in design and communicated to fab:

| Rule | Value | Verified |
|------|-------|----------|
| Min trace width | 0.15mm (6 mil) | [ ] |
| Min trace space | 0.15mm (6 mil) | [ ] |
| Min via drill | 0.3mm (12 mil) | [ ] |
| Min annular ring | 0.15mm (6 mil) | [ ] |
| Outer copper | 2 oz (70 µm) | [ ] |
| Inner copper | 1 oz (35 µm) | [ ] |
| Board thickness | 1.6mm | [ ] |
| Surface finish | ENIG | [ ] |
| Solder mask | Green or Black | [ ] |
| Silkscreen | White | [ ] |

### 5. Special Manufacturing Notes
- [ ] Controlled impedance: Not required
- [ ] Via fill: Required for thermal pads (specify in notes)
- [ ] Edge plating: Not required
- [ ] Slots: Specify in drill file or separate file
- [ ] Panel: Individual board (no panel)

### 6. Fab House Pre-Check (if available)
- [ ] Upload to fab house DFM checker
- [ ] Review any warnings
- [ ] Resolve or accept warnings with justification
- [ ] Quote matches expectations

### 7. Revision Control
- [ ] Design version correct on board
- [ ] Design version in filename
- [ ] Git commit tagged with release
- [ ] Change log updated

### 8. Final Checklist Completion
- [ ] Schematic review checklist complete (REQ-REV-01)
- [ ] Layout review checklist complete (REQ-REV-02)
- [ ] All blocking issues resolved
- [ ] All warnings documented and accepted

## Fabrication Order Information

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Board Name | TEMPER v1.0 |
| Quantity | [To be determined] |
| Layer Count | 4 |
| Dimensions | 100mm × 150mm |
| Material | FR4 TG150 |
| Copper | 2oz/1oz/1oz/1oz |
| Finish | ENIG |
| Solder Mask | [Green/Black] |
| Silkscreen | White |
| Lead Time | [Standard/Rush] |

## Sign-Off

**I confirm that this design has been reviewed and is ready for fabrication:**

| Role | Name | Date | Signature |
|------|------|------|-----------|
| Design Engineer | | | |
| Project Lead | | | |

## Deliverables
- [ ] Gerber package (ZIP file)
- [ ] Drill file
- [ ] BOM (final version)
- [ ] Pick-and-place file
- [ ] Assembly drawing
- [ ] README with fabrication notes

## Acceptance Criteria
- All verification steps completed
- All sign-offs obtained
- Files uploaded to fab house
- Order confirmation received

title: temper-placer: Comprehensive Test Suite for PCB Generation Pipeline
description: Systematic test suite to verify the PCB generation pipeline works correctly end-to-end, handles real-world edge cases gracefully, and produces valid output. Based on gap analysis: CLI optimize command has no integration test (would have caught the temperature.start bug), parser has no malformed input handling tests, no roundtrip verification, and no stress tests for larger PCBs. Goal: confidence that optimization works for real-world PCBs, not just minimal fixtures.

title: CLI optimize command integration test
description:
Create tests/cli/test_optimize_command.py with full integration tests for the optimize CLI command. This is the HIGHEST priority because the bug we just fixed (temperature.initial → temperature.start on line 249) would have been caught by this test.

Tests to implement:
1. test_optimize_minimal_board - Run optimize on minimal_board.kicad_pcb, verify exit code 0, output file exists, loss decreases
2. test_optimize_with_all_flags - Test --learning-rate, --epochs, --seed, --verbose, --checkpoint flags
3. test_optimize_missing_input - Non-existent input file should exit non-zero with helpful error
4. test_optimize_invalid_constraints - Malformed YAML should fail gracefully
5. test_optimize_creates_checkpoint - Verify --checkpoint flag creates checkpoint file
6. test_optimize_resume_from_checkpoint - Test resuming optimization from checkpoint
7. test_optimize_output_is_valid_kicad - Re-parse output file to verify it's valid

Use click.testing.CliRunner for all tests. Fixtures: tests/fixtures/minimal_board.kicad_pcb, tests/fixtures/constraints_minimal.yaml

title: CLI error handling and edge case tests
description:
Create tests/cli/test_cli_error_handling.py to verify graceful error handling for all CLI commands.

Good CLI tools fail gracefully with helpful error messages. Currently untested.

Tests to implement:
1. test_optimize_nonexistent_file - Clear error message, exit code 1
2. test_optimize_directory_not_file - Passing a directory instead of file
3. test_optimize_permission_denied - Can't write to output location
4. test_optimize_disk_full - Simulate disk full (mock)
5. test_config_yaml_syntax_error - Invalid YAML syntax
6. test_config_missing_required_field - YAML missing board dimensions
7. test_config_invalid_values - Negative board size, etc.
8. test_info_nonexistent_file - info command with bad file
9. test_report_invalid_format - Invalid --format option
10. test_keyboard_interrupt - Ctrl+C during optimization handled cleanly
11. test_help_all_commands - Every command has --help

Verify:
- Exit code is non-zero for errors
- Error message is human-readable
- No stack traces in normal error cases
- Stack trace available with --verbose or --debug

title: Real-world PCB integration tests
description:
Create tests/integration/test_real_pcbs.py to test with production-quality open-source KiCad projects.

Testing with synthetic fixtures misses real-world complexity. Download and test against real hardware designs from reputable open-source projects.

## External PCB Sources (via manifest.yaml)
1. beekeeb/piantor - Split keyboard (~50-80 components)
2. skot/bitaxe - Bitcoin ASIC miner with power electronics (~80-150 components)
3. scottbez1/splitflap - Split-flap display driver (~40-80 components)
4. but0n/Avem - Drone flight controller (~100-200 components)
5. espressif/esp-dev-kits - ESP32-S3 reference design (~50-100 components)

## Infrastructure Required
- tests/fixtures/external/manifest.yaml - PCB source definitions
- tests/fixtures/external/download_pcbs.py - Download/cache utility
- tests/fixtures/external/constraint_generator.py - Generate constraints from parsed boards

## Tests for each PCB
1. test_parse_<name> - Parses without errors
2. test_optimize_<name> - Optimization completes  
3. test_export_<name> - Export produces valid KiCad file
4. test_loss_improves_<name> - Loss actually decreases

Mark slow tests with @pytest.mark.slow. Skip tests gracefully if PCB not downloaded.

title: CLI report command tests
description:
Create tests/cli/test_report_command.py to test the report generation CLI command.

The report command generates HTML visualization reports. Currently has no CLI-level tests.

Tests to implement:
1. test_report_basic - Generate report for minimal_board, verify HTML output
2. test_report_with_drc - --drc flag includes DRC results (requires kicad-cli)
3. test_report_output_path - Custom --output path works
4. test_report_formats - Test different output formats if supported
5. test_report_includes_loss_history - Loss plot included in report
6. test_report_includes_board_visualization - Board rendering present
7. test_report_opens_browser - --open flag behavior (may need mock)
8. test_report_from_checkpoint - Generate report from checkpoint file
9. test_report_missing_optimization - Report without running optimize first

Verify HTML output:
- Contains expected sections (Summary, Board, Losses)
- Images/plots are embedded or linked correctly
- No broken references

title: External PCB manifest system
description:
Create tests/fixtures/external/manifest.yaml that defines open-source KiCad projects to download for testing.

Each entry specifies:
- repo: GitHub repository (owner/name)
- ref: Git ref for reproducibility
- pcb_files: List of PCB files to extract
- license: SPDX license identifier
- complexity: small/medium/large

Projects to include:
- beekeeb/piantor (keyboard)
- skot/bitaxe (Bitcoin miner, power electronics)
- scottbez1/splitflap (display driver)
- but0n/Avem (flight controller)
- espressif/esp-dev-kits (reference design)

title: PCB download/cache utility
description:
Create tests/fixtures/external/download_pcbs.py that:

1. Parses manifest.yaml to get project definitions
2. Downloads PCB files from GitHub (sparse checkout or raw file fetch)
3. Caches downloaded files in .cache/ directory
4. Provides CLI: python -m tests.fixtures.external.download_pcbs [--project NAME] [--all]
5. Exposes get_pcb_path(project_name) for test fixtures

Features:
- Only downloads if not already cached
- Verifies file integrity (basic size check)
- Handles network errors gracefully
- Supports --force flag to re-download

title: Constraint generator for real PCBs
description:
Create tests/fixtures/external/constraint_generator.py that generates realistic constraint YAML files from parsed PCB boards.

Given a parsed PCB (via kicad_parser), generate constraints including:
- Board dimensions from Edge.Cuts layer
- Component zones based on functional groupings (power, signal, connectors)
- Keep-out areas if defined in PCB
- Net class weights based on net names (power nets weighted higher)
- Optimization parameters scaled to board complexity

Function: generate_constraints(parse_result: ParseResult) -> dict

title: Real PCB parsing tests
description:
Add parsing tests to tests/integration/test_real_pcbs.py:

- test_parse_piantor_keyboard
- test_parse_bitaxe_miner  
- test_parse_splitflap_chainlink
- test_parse_avem_flight_controller
- test_parse_esp32_devkit

Each test verifies:
1. PCB parses without exceptions
2. Board dimensions are extracted
3. Component count matches expected range
4. Nets are extracted correctly

Use @pytest.mark.external to skip if PCB not downloaded.

title: Real PCB optimization tests
description:
Add optimization tests to tests/integration/test_real_pcbs.py:

- test_optimize_piantor_keyboard
- test_optimize_bitaxe_miner (mark @pytest.mark.slow)
- test_optimize_splitflap_chainlink
- test_optimize_avem_flight_controller (mark @pytest.mark.slow)
- test_optimize_esp32_devkit

Each test verifies:
1. Optimization completes without exceptions
2. Returns valid PlacementState
3. All components have valid positions within board bounds
4. Runtime is reasonable for board complexity

Use generated constraints from constraint_generator.py.

title: Real PCB export/roundtrip tests
description:
Add export and roundtrip tests to tests/integration/test_real_pcbs.py:

- test_export_<name> - Export optimized placement to KiCad file
- test_roundtrip_<name> - Re-parse exported file, verify positions match

Each test verifies:
1. Export produces valid KiCad PCB file
2. Exported file can be re-parsed
3. Component count preserved
4. Positions match within tolerance (0.01mm)
5. Rotations preserved correctly

title: Real PCB loss improvement tests
description:
Add loss improvement verification tests to tests/integration/test_real_pcbs.py:

- test_loss_improves_<name> for each external PCB

Each test verifies:
1. Initial loss is computed correctly
2. Final loss < initial loss (optimization improves placement)
3. Loss is finite (no NaN/Inf)
4. Best loss tracked correctly in checkpoint

These tests validate that the optimizer actually improves real-world designs, not just synthetic test cases.

title: Parser robustness tests for malformed KiCad files
description:
Create tests/robustness/test_malformed_input.py to verify the parser handles invalid input gracefully without crashing.

Currently, parse_kicad_pcb() has minimal error handling (only one try/except at line 661). Malformed files will cause unhandled exceptions.

Tests to implement:
1. test_empty_file - Empty file should raise clear ParseError, not crash
2. test_truncated_file - S-expression cut off mid-parse should fail gracefully
3. test_binary_garbage - Binary data should raise ParseError, not UnicodeDecodeError
4. test_invalid_sexpr_syntax - Mismatched parentheses should fail gracefully
5. test_missing_board_outline - PCB with no Edge.Cuts should warn but parse components
6. test_component_missing_position - Footprint without 'at' attribute should be skipped with warning
7. test_component_missing_footprint - Component entry without footprint data should be skipped
8. test_unicode_component_refs - Refs with μ, Ω, etc. should parse correctly
9. test_very_long_component_ref - Stress test with 1000+ char ref name
10. test_negative_coordinates - Components at negative positions should parse

Create malformed fixture files in tests/fixtures/malformed/ directory.

Expected behavior: ParseError for fatal issues, warnings list for recoverable issues, never unhandled exceptions.

title: Pipeline roundtrip verification tests
description:
Create tests/integration/test_roundtrip.py to verify data integrity through the full parse→optimize→export→re-parse cycle.

This catches subtle bugs where data is lost or corrupted during serialization.

Tests to implement:
1. test_position_roundtrip - Known positions survive export→re-parse within 0.01mm tolerance
2. test_rotation_roundtrip - All 4 rotation angles (0°, 90°, 180°, 270°) survive roundtrip
3. test_rotation_negative_angles - Negative angles like -90° normalize correctly
4. test_net_preservation - Net assignments preserved after roundtrip
5. test_trace_preservation - Existing traces in PCB not corrupted by placement export
6. test_zone_preservation - Copper zones preserved after export
7. test_metadata_preservation - KiCad version, generator info preserved
8. test_layer_preservation - Layer assignments (F.Cu, B.Cu) preserved
9. test_component_count_preserved - Same number of components before/after
10. test_idempotent_export - Exporting twice produces identical files

Key verification: compare ParseResult.netlist before and after, component by component.

title: CLI export and validate command tests
description:
Create tests/cli/test_export_command.py and tests/cli/test_validate_command.py.

The export and validate CLI commands have no integration tests currently.

Export command tests:
1. test_export_json_format - Export state to JSON, verify structure
2. test_export_kicad_format - Export to .kicad_pcb, verify file valid
3. test_export_preserves_template - Non-placement data preserved from template
4. test_export_missing_template - Missing template file fails gracefully
5. test_export_mismatched_refs - Component ref mismatch handled

Validate command tests:
1. test_validate_runs_drc - DRC check executes (may need kicad-cli)
2. test_validate_reports_violations - Overlapping components flagged
3. test_validate_clean_board - Valid placement passes validation
4. test_validate_without_kicad_cli - Graceful error if kicad-cli missing
5. test_validate_json_output - --json flag produces parseable output

Use click.testing.CliRunner. Mark tests requiring kicad-cli with @pytest.mark.skipif decorator.

title: Create medium-complexity test fixture (25-30 components)
description:
Create tests/fixtures/medium_board.kicad_pcb with 25-30 components to bridge the gap between minimal (4 components) and real-world (100+).

Current fixtures:
- minimal_board.kicad_pcb: 4 components (R1, R2, C1, U1)
- 08_MOSFET_Driver.kicad_pcb: 17 components (external, may not always be available)

Medium fixture should include:
- Mix of component types: resistors (10), capacitors (8), ICs (3), connectors (2), inductors (2)
- Realistic net structure: power nets (VCC, GND), signal nets, differential pairs
- Various footprint sizes: 0402, 0603, 0805, SOT-23, SOIC-8, QFP
- Some components pre-placed, some at origin (simulates partial placement)
- Board size: ~60x40mm

Also create tests/fixtures/constraints_medium.yaml with:
- 2-3 placement zones
- Keep-out areas
- Component grouping constraints

This fixture enables meaningful integration tests without the overhead of 100+ components.

title: Optimization correctness verification tests
description:
Create tests/verification/test_placement_correctness.py to verify optimization actually produces valid, improved placements.

Current verification tests check gradients and numerical stability, but don't verify the optimization RESULT is correct.

Tests to implement:
1. test_loss_decreases_significantly - Final loss < 50% of initial loss (proves optimization working)
2. test_no_overlaps_final_placement - Overlap loss essentially zero after optimization
3. test_all_components_within_bounds - Boundary loss essentially zero
4. test_wirelength_finite - Wirelength loss is finite and reasonable
5. test_positions_changed - Components actually moved from initial positions
6. test_positions_diverse - Not all components collapsed to same point
7. test_rotations_valid - All rotations are one of [0, 90, 180, 270]
8. test_reproducible_with_seed - Same seed produces same result
9. test_different_seeds_different_results - Different seeds explore different solutions
10. test_curriculum_improves_result - Curriculum learning beats fixed temperature

These tests verify the GOAL is achieved, not just that the code runs.

title: Numerical edge case and stability tests
description:
Create tests/robustness/test_numerical_edge_cases.py to verify numerical stability under extreme conditions.

The JAX-based optimizer can produce NaN/Inf in edge cases. Current tests check gradient stability but not these specific triggers.

Tests to implement:
1. test_nan_detection_overlapping_init - Overlapping initial positions don't produce NaN
2. test_inf_gradient_at_boundary - Component exactly at boundary has finite gradient
3. test_zero_area_component - Component with 0 width/height handled
4. test_huge_coordinates - Positions at 1e6 mm don't overflow
5. test_tiny_coordinates - Positions at 1e-6 mm don't underflow
6. test_single_pin_net_wirelength - Net with 1 pin doesn't cause division by zero
7. test_all_components_same_position - Degenerate case handled
8. test_learning_rate_extremes - Very high/low LR doesn't crash
9. test_temperature_zero - Temperature=0 in Gumbel-Softmax handled
10. test_negative_component_size - Negative bounds don't cause issues

For each test, verify: no NaN, no Inf, finite loss, finite gradients.

title: Performance scaling tests (10-200 components)
description:
Create tests/performance/test_scaling.py to measure and verify performance scaling.

Currently no tests verify the optimizer handles larger PCBs efficiently. The Temper board has ~100 components.

Tests to implement (marked @pytest.mark.slow):
1. test_optimization_time_10_components - Baseline, should complete in <10s
2. test_optimization_time_25_components - Should complete in <30s
3. test_optimization_time_50_components - Should complete in <60s  
4. test_optimization_time_100_components - Target: <5 minutes (see temper-7t1.9.9.7)
5. test_optimization_time_200_components - Stress test, <15 minutes
6. test_scaling_is_reasonable - Time should scale O(n²) or better, not O(n³)
7. test_memory_usage_100_components - Peak memory <2GB
8. test_jit_compilation_time - First epoch vs subsequent (JIT overhead)

Use synthetic netlists generated programmatically. Log timing results for benchmarking.

Mark all with @pytest.mark.slow so they're skipped in quick test runs.
Run with: pytest tests/performance/ -v --slow

title: Create large test fixture (100+ components)
description:
Create tests/fixtures/large_board.kicad_pcb with 100+ components for stress testing.

This matches the scale of the actual Temper induction cooker board (~100 components).

Approach: Generate programmatically using a Python script (tests/fixtures/generate_large_board.py):
- 50 resistors (0402, 0603, 0805 mix)
- 30 capacitors (various sizes)
- 10 ICs (SOT-23, SOIC-8, QFP-32, QFP-48)
- 5 connectors
- 5 inductors/ferrites

Net structure:
- VCC and GND power rails connecting to most components
- 20+ signal nets with 2-5 pins each
- 2-3 differential pairs
- Some unconnected pins (realistic)

Board: 100x150mm (matches Temper spec)

Also create tests/fixtures/constraints_large.yaml with:
- HV zone, control zone, power zone, sensing zone
- Keep-out for mounting holes
- HV clearance requirements

This enables realistic performance benchmarking and stress testing.

title: EPIC: Critical Safety Issues - Fire/Explosion Hazards
description:
This epic tracks the 3 CRITICAL safety issues identified in the counterfactual analysis that could cause fire, explosion, or component destruction. These MUST be resolved before any hardware is built.

## Critical Issues

### CRITICAL-1: Resonant Capacitor Explosion Risk
- Severity: FIRE HAZARD
- Status: CONFIRMED - Negative margin in simulation
- Capacitor voltage rating: 630V
- Simulated peak voltage: 648V (-2.9% margin)
- Failure mode: Dielectric stress → degradation → short/venting → fire

### CRITICAL-2: IGBT Desaturation Not Detected
- Severity: COMPONENT DESTRUCTION  
- UCC21550 has desaturation detection but NOT configured
- Failure mode: Shoot-through/short → >100A in linear region → destruction

### CRITICAL-3: PLL Lock Loss → Hard Switching → Thermal Runaway
- Severity: THERMAL DESTRUCTION
- No PLL lock detection in firmware
- Failure mode: PLL diverges → hard switching → 10-100x switching losses → thermal runaway

## Success Criteria
- No component operating above rated voltage
- All fault detection mechanisms implemented and tested
- Firmware detects and safely handles loss of ZVS

## Priority
P0 - MUST FIX BEFORE HARDWARE BUILD
close_reason: All sub-tasks (Capacitor upgrade, DESAT configuration, PLL/ZVS logic) are complete and verified.

title: CRITICAL-1: Upgrade Resonant Capacitor to ≥700V Rating
description:
## Problem
The resonant capacitor is already operating above its voltage rating - this is a FIRE HAZARD.

| Parameter | Specified | Simulated | Margin |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Capacitor voltage rating | 630V | 648V peak | -2.9% |

## Failure Scenario
1. Capacitor operates above rated voltage every switching cycle
2. Dielectric stress causes gradual degradation
3. Internal short or venting occurs
4. Fire or explosion in enclosed chassis

## Required Actions

### Immediate
- Update BOM with capacitor rated ≥700V (50% margin over 648V peak)
- Select film capacitor with appropriate ripple current rating (≥15A RMS at 38kHz)
- Verify physical dimensions fit existing layout
- Update simulation with new capacitor parameters

### Recommended Part Selection Criteria
- Voltage Rating: ≥700V DC (prefer 800V or 1000V)
- Capacitance: Match existing value (likely 100nF-470nF based on resonant freq)
- Dielectric: Polypropylene film (PP) - best for high-frequency, high-voltage
- Ripple Current: ≥15A RMS at 38kHz
- Package: Radial or axial, check mechanical fit

### Candidate Parts (verify availability/specs)
- KEMET R76 series (1000V DC)
- Vishay MKP series (800-1000V)
- EPCOS B32774 series (800V)
- Cornell Dubilier 940C series (800V)

## Verification
- Simulation confirms peak voltage with 20% margin to new rating
- Thermal analysis confirms adequate cooling
- Physical fit verified in mechanical design

## Risk if Not Fixed
**Cannot ship hardware. Liability exposure. Potential kitchen fire.**

title: CRITICAL-2: Configure UCC21550 Desaturation Detection
description:
## Problem
The UCC21550 gate driver has desaturation detection capability, but it's NOT documented or configured in the design.

## Failure Scenario
1. IGBT enters desaturation during fault (e.g., shoot-through, short circuit)
2. IGBT conducts massive current (>100A) in linear region
3. OCP (33ns response) is designed for tank current, not IGBT desaturation
4. IGBT junction reaches 175°C in microseconds → destruction
5. Possible case rupture, arc flash

## Required Actions

### Hardware
1. Review UCC21550 datasheet DESAT detection section
2. Design DESAT blanking time circuit (typically 100-500ns)
3. Select DESAT threshold voltage (typically 7-9V for IGBT VCE(sat))
4. Add required external components:
   - DESAT diode (fast recovery, >600V rating)
   - Blanking capacitor (sets blanking time)
   - Threshold resistor (if adjustable)
5. Route DESAT pin to IGBT collector through HV diode
6. Configure FAULT output handling

### Firmware
1. Add FAULT pin interrupt handler
2. Implement soft-shutdown sequence on DESAT fault
3. Add fault logging (timestamp, fault type)
4. Define fault recovery procedure (manual reset required)

### Circuit Design Parameters
IGBT VCE(sat) @ 40A: ~2.5V (from IKW40N120H3 datasheet)
DESAT threshold: 7V (safe margin above VCE(sat))
Blanking time: 300ns (avoid false triggers during switching)
DESAT diode: STTH1R06 (600V, 1A, fast recovery)
Blanking cap: Calculate for 300ns with UCC21550 DESAT current

## Schematic Updates Required
- Add DESAT circuit to gate driver schematic
- Add FAULT output connection to ESP32
- Update BOM with new components

## Verification
- Simulate DESAT response with SPICE
- Test DESAT detection with bench supply forcing VCE high
- Verify blanking time prevents false triggers
- Verify FAULT response time < 2µs

## Risk if Not Fixed
**IGBT destruction on first fault. Potential arc flash, PCB fire.**

title: CRITICAL-3: Implement PLL Lock Detection and ZVS Verification
description:
## Problem
The firmware has NO PLL lock detection. If PLL loses lock, catastrophic thermal failure occurs.

## Failure Scenario
1. PLL algorithm diverges (noise, pan removal, frequency drift)
2. Operating frequency sweeps below resonance
3. Half-bridge enters hard switching mode (no ZVS)
4. Switching losses increase 10-100x
5. IGBT junction temperature rises rapidly
6. Thermal protection (85°C heatsink) is too slow to prevent junction damage

## Why Current Protection is Insufficient
- NTC on heatsink lags junction by 10-20°C
- Thermal time constant: seconds (too slow for fast thermal runaway)
- No direct detection of hard switching condition

## Required Actions

### Firmware - PLL Lock Detection
1. Define PLL lock criteria:
   - Phase error < ±15° for >10 consecutive cycles
   - Frequency within ±2kHz of expected resonant frequency
   - Current waveform zero-crossing aligned with voltage

2. Implement lock detection algorithm with lock_fail_count tracking
3. Add frequency boundary checking:
   - Minimum frequency: f_res - 5kHz (hard limit)
   - Maximum frequency: f_res + 10kHz (allows inductive margin)
   - Outside bounds → immediate shutdown

### Firmware - ZVS Verification
1. Monitor switching node voltage at turn-on:
   - Sample V_SW just before high-side turn-on
   - If V_SW > 50V, ZVS is not achieved
   - Track consecutive hard-switching events

2. Implement ZVS loss response:
   - 1-3 hard switches: Log warning, increase dead time
   - >3 consecutive: Reduce power 50%
   - >10 consecutive: Shutdown with fault

### Hardware Support (if needed)
- Add ZVS detection comparator (optional HW backup)
- Compare V_SW to threshold (50V)
- Output to ESP32 GPIO

## Test Procedure
- Simulate pan removal during operation
- Inject frequency disturbance
- Verify lock-loss detection < 200ms
- Verify safe shutdown sequence

## Verification Criteria
- PLL unlock detected within 5 switching cycles (130µs at 38kHz)
- Frequency excursion beyond limits → immediate shutdown
- Hard switching detected within 1ms
- Safe state entered before junction temp exceeds 125°C

## Risk if Not Fixed
**Silent failure mode. IGBT thermal destruction during normal operation.**
notes:
Implemented comprehensive PLL lock detection and ZVS verification:
1. Enhanced PLL lock detection with consecutive cycle tracking (10 cycles, ±15° phase error, ±2kHz freq tolerance)
2. Frequency boundary hard limits (f_res ± margins)
3. ZVS hardware monitoring framework with graduated response (warning→power reduction→shutdown)
4. Safety system integration with new fault codes
5. Created detailed integration guide: docs/PLL_ZVS_INTEGRATION_GUIDE.md
Hardware integration (V_SW ADC sensing circuit) required before testing.

title: Optimizer Validation Epic: Ensure Real-World Placement Quality
description:
Meta-epic tracking all validation improvements to ensure the placer produces manufacturable, high-quality PCB placements.

## Current Status (2025-12-17)

**Validation Approach**: Using external open-source PCBs as quality references instead of manual placements.

**Test Results** (tests/comparison/test_ground_truth_comparison.py):
- ✅ 6/8 tests passing (75%)
- ✅ Wirelength optimization: 4/4 projects within 150% of human baseline
- ⚠️ Hard constraints: 2/4 projects have overlap/boundary violations >10.0

**Reference PCBs**:
- piantor_left/right (36 components, keyboards)
- bitaxe_ultra (137 components, Bitcoin miner) ✅ PASSES ALL
- rp2040_designguide (36 components, dev board) ✅ PASSES ALL
- libresolar_bms (209 components, not tested yet)

**Key Findings**:
1. Optimizer produces competitive wirelength (within 150% of human)
2. Optimizer improves over human baselines on overlap/boundary (e.g., piantor_left: 276→84 overlap, 48948→71 boundary)
3. Some designs don't reach <10.0 threshold with 200 epochs
4. Human baselines have significant violations, suggesting pre-routed constraints

**Known Issues**:
- temper-aw8: piantor_left overlap 62.5 (threshold <10.0)
- temper-a63: piantor_right boundary 54.0 (threshold <10.0)

**Next Steps**:
1. Fix tests to use heuristics initialization + more epochs (1000-2000)
2. Tune loss weights (may need >1000 for stricter convergence)
3. Consider adaptive thresholds based on human baseline quality
4. Scale testing with libresolar_bms (209 components)

See OPTIMIZER_VALIDATION_REPORT.md for full details.

title: Create footprint library with real bounds for Temper components
description:
Create a footprint bounds library for all Temper board components.

## Scope
Extract accurate courtyard bounds for all ~95 Temper components from KiCad footprint files.

## Target Components (by category)

### Power Stage (Critical - large footprints)
- Q1, Q2: IKW40N120H3 (TO-247-3) — 16.0 x 21.0mm courtyard
- D1-D4: Rectifier diodes (DO-247) — verify actual footprint
- U_GATE_DRIVER: UCC21550 (SOIC-16) — 10.3 x 7.5mm

### Passives (High count)
- Bulk capacitors: 450V electrolytics — diameter + lead spacing
- Decoupling caps: 0805, 0603 — 2.0x1.25mm, 1.6x0.8mm
- Resistors: 0805, 0603, 2512 (power) — standard bounds
- Current sense: R_SENSE_HIGH, R_SENSE_LOW — 2512 or custom

### Control (Medium complexity)
- U_MCU: ESP32-S3 module — verify module footprint vs chip
- U_TEMP_SENSE: MAX31865 (TSSOP-20) — 6.5 x 4.4mm
- Y1: Crystal (HC49 or SMD) — varies by part

### Connectors
- J_AC_IN: IEC inlet — large, asymmetric
- J_COIL: High-current connector
- J_PROBE: Thermocouple connector

## Output Format
YAML file at `temper-placer/config/footprint_library.yaml`:

```yaml
footprints:
  TO-247-3:
    bounds: [16.0, 21.0]
    courtyard_margin: 0.25
    thermal_pad: true
    pin_1_offset: [-5.08, 0]
    
  SOIC-16_W:
    bounds: [10.3, 7.5]
    courtyard_margin: 0.2
    thermal_pad: false
    
  "0805":
    bounds: [2.0, 1.25]
    courtyard_margin: 0.15
```

## Verification
- Cross-reference with KiCad footprint library
- Verify 3 random footprints against actual .kicad_mod files
- Bounds should match courtyard, not just pads

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All ~95 Temper components have entries
- [ ] Bounds accurate to ±0.1mm vs KiCad courtyard
- [ ] Thermal pad flag set correctly for power components
- [ ] YAML loads without error in config_loader.py

title: Update test netlists to use accurate footprint bounds
description:
Update all test netlists to use accurate footprint bounds from the library.

## Scope
Replace hardcoded bounds in test fixtures with values from footprint_library.yaml.

## Files to Update

### Unit Test Fixtures
- `tests/fixtures/thermal_netlist.py` — Q1, Q2 bounds
- `tests/fixtures/simple_netlist.py` — generic components
- `tests/losses/test_*.py` — inline Component() definitions

### Integration Test Fixtures  
- `tests/fixtures/temper_netlist.yaml` — full Temper board
- `tests/comparison/fixtures/*.yaml` — comparison test configs

### External PCB Fixtures (verify, don't change)
- `tests/fixtures/external/*/constraints.yaml` — should already use real bounds

## Implementation

1. Add footprint library loader to test conftest.py:
```python
@pytest.fixture
def footprint_library():
    return load_footprint_library("config/footprint_library.yaml")

@pytest.fixture  
def component_factory(footprint_library):
    def make_component(ref: str, footprint: str, **kwargs):
        bounds = footprint_library[footprint]["bounds"]
        return Component(ref=ref, footprint=footprint, bounds=bounds, **kwargs)
    return make_component
```

2. Update each test file to use factory instead of hardcoded bounds

3. Add validation that bounds match library on test startup

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] No hardcoded bounds in test files (grep for `bounds=`)
- [ ] All tests pass with library-loaded bounds
- [ ] Test startup validates bounds consistency
- [ ] CI catches bounds drift (library vs test mismatch)

title: Sub-epic: Constraint Interaction Testing
description:
Test that multiple constraints work together correctly and degrade gracefully when conflicting.

Current gap: Each constraint tested in isolation. Multi-objective optimization might sacrifice one constraint for another unexpectedly.

Example conflicts:
- Thermal constraint pushes IGBTs to edge, but this might violate HV-LV clearance
- Zone membership vs wirelength optimization
- Dense placement vs minimum spacing

Goal: Adversarial test cases verify graceful degradation and appropriate trade-offs.

title: Test: Thermal vs HV-LV clearance conflict
description:
TDD Task: Test behavior when thermal constraints conflict with clearance rules.

Scenario:
- IGBTs must be at TOP edge (thermal constraint)
- LV components also at TOP edge
- HV-LV clearance requires 10mm separation

Expected behavior:
- Optimizer finds compromise (IGBTs near but not AT edge)
- OR optimizer fails gracefully with clear diagnostic

Test file: tests/verification/test_constraint_conflicts.py

Tests:
1. test_thermal_clearance_conflict_setup - Create conflicting constraints
2. test_conflict_produces_valid_placement - No overlaps, within bounds
3. test_conflict_reports_tradeoff - Loss breakdown shows which constraint sacrificed
4. test_conflict_with_impossible_constraints - Board too small for all constraints

title: Test: Zone membership vs wirelength conflict
description:
TDD Task: Test when zone constraints conflict with wirelength optimization.

Scenario:
- Component A assigned to HV_ZONE (left side)
- Component B assigned to LV_ZONE (right side)
- Net connects A and B (wants them close)

Expected behavior:
- Components stay in assigned zones
- Wirelength is minimized WITHIN zone constraints
- Components placed at zone boundaries facing each other

Tests:
1. test_zone_wirelength_conflict_setup
2. test_components_remain_in_zones
3. test_wirelength_minimized_within_zones
4. test_boundary_placement_optimization

title: Test: Impossible constraint detection
description:
TDD Task: Test that optimizer detects and reports impossible constraints.

Scenarios:
1. Board too small for all components (physical impossibility)
2. Zone bounds don't fit assigned components
3. Conflicting clearance rules (A must be near B, but also 20mm away)
4. Thermal constraints requiring component outside board

Expected behavior:
- Preflight check catches obvious impossibilities
- Optimizer returns diagnostic when convergence fails
- Clear error messages explaining which constraints conflict

Tests in: tests/verification/test_impossible_constraints.py

title: Sub-epic: Scale Testing
description:
Verify optimizer behavior at realistic component counts.

## Status Update (2025-12-17)
- Libresolar BMS (209 components) failed validation (2.26x baseline).
- Root cause: Random initialization trapped in local minima.
- Plan: Implement Spectral Initialization (temper-1my.7) to improve global structure.

Concerns:
- Convergence speed at scale
- Quality degradation with more components
- Memory usage
- Gradient vanishing/exploding

Goal: Demonstrate quality placement for 50, 100, 200+ component designs.
close_reason: Scale testing successful. Spectral initialization addresses the random init local minima bottleneck for 200+ components.

title: Generate synthetic netlist with 200 components
description:
Generate a synthetic 200-component netlist for scale testing.

## Purpose
Test optimizer behavior at the upper end of realistic PCB complexity. 200 components is typical for:
- Complex mixed-signal boards
- Power electronics with extensive filtering
- IoT gateways with multiple interfaces

## Netlist Specifications

### Component Distribution (realistic mix)
| Category | Count | Footprints |
|----------|-------|------------|
| Resistors | 80 | 0603 (60), 0805 (15), 2512 (5) |
| Capacitors | 50 | 0603 (30), 0805 (15), electrolytic (5) |
| ICs | 25 | QFN (10), SOIC (10), TSSOP (5) |
| Inductors | 10 | 0805 (5), power (5) |
| Connectors | 15 | various |
| Discretes | 20 | SOT-23 (15), TO-220 (5) |

### Connectivity Pattern
Use realistic net distribution (not random):
- **Power nets**: +3V3, +5V, +12V, GND — high fanout (20-50 pins each)
- **Signal nets**: 2-4 pins each, ~100 nets
- **Bus nets**: I2C, SPI — 5-10 pins each
- **Differential pairs**: 5 pairs

```python
def generate_200_component_netlist():
    # Power distribution network
    gnd_net = Net("GND", pins=[...])  # ~80 pins
    vcc_net = Net("+3V3", pins=[...])  # ~40 pins
    
    # Signal nets with realistic fanout
    signal_nets = [
        Net(f"SIG_{i}", pins=random_pins(2, 4))
        for i in range(100)
    ]
    
    # I2C bus
    i2c_sda = Net("I2C_SDA", pins=[...])  # 8 pins
    i2c_scl = Net("I2C_SCL", pins=[...])  # 8 pins
```

### Board Dimensions
- 150mm x 100mm (typical 4-layer board)
- Origin at (0, 0)

## Output
- `tests/fixtures/synthetic/netlist_200.yaml`
- `tests/fixtures/synthetic/netlist_200_constraints.yaml`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Exactly 200 components
- [ ] Realistic footprint distribution (see table)
- [ ] Power nets have high fanout (>20 pins)
- [ ] Signal nets have low fanout (2-4 pins)
- [ ] Netlist loads and parses without error
- [ ] Optimizer completes 1000 epochs in <60 seconds (M1 Mac)

title: Profile memory usage at scale
description:
Profile memory usage at scale to identify bottlenecks and set thresholds.

## Purpose
Ensure optimizer can handle large boards without OOM on typical development machines (16GB RAM).

## Profiling Methodology

### Tool: memory_profiler + JAX memory stats
```python
from memory_profiler import profile
import jax

@profile
def run_optimization(netlist, epochs):
    # ... optimization loop
    pass

# JAX-specific memory tracking
jax.profiler.save_device_memory_profile("memory.prof")
```

### Test Matrix
| Components | Board Size | Epochs | Expected Peak RAM |
|------------|------------|--------|-------------------|
| 50 | 80x60mm | 2000 | <500MB |
| 100 | 100x80mm | 2000 | <1GB |
| 200 | 150x100mm | 2000 | <2GB |
| 500 | 200x150mm | 2000 | <4GB |

### Metrics to Capture
1. **Peak RSS** — maximum resident set size
2. **JAX device memory** — GPU/TPU allocation (if used)
3. **Memory growth rate** — MB per 100 epochs
4. **GC pressure** — number of collections during run

### Memory Breakdown by Component
Identify which data structures consume most memory:
- Position arrays: N x 2 x float32
- Rotation arrays: N x 4 x float32
- Distance matrices: N x N x float32 (quadratic!)
- Gradient buffers: same as positions
- Loss history: epochs x num_losses

## Implementation

1. Create `scripts/profile_memory.py`:
```bash
python scripts/profile_memory.py --components 200 --epochs 2000 --output memory_report.json
```

2. Add pytest markers for memory tests:
```python
@pytest.mark.memory
@pytest.mark.parametrize("n_components", [50, 100, 200])
def test_memory_scaling(n_components):
    ...
```

3. Add CI memory regression test (fail if peak > threshold)

## Thresholds (Fail CI if exceeded)
- 100 components: 1.5GB peak
- 200 components: 3GB peak
- Memory growth: <1MB per 100 epochs (no leaks)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Memory profiling script exists and runs
- [ ] Profiling results for 50, 100, 200, 500 components documented
- [ ] Memory thresholds defined and enforced in CI
- [ ] No memory leaks (growth rate < 1MB/100 epochs)
- [ ] Quadratic memory (distance matrices) identified and documented

title: Sub-epic: Seed Sensitivity Analysis
description:
Quantify optimizer variance across random initializations.

Current state: Tests use fixed seeds for reproducibility. Real use cases start from random init.

Questions to answer:
- What is the variance in final loss across 100 random seeds?
- What is the variance in quality metrics (overlap, boundary, wirelength)?
- Are there pathological seeds that fail to converge?
- How does initialization strategy affect outcomes?

Goal: Demonstrate low variance across seeds, identify failure modes.

title: Monte Carlo test with 100 random seeds
description:
TDD Task: Statistical test of optimizer variance across seeds.

Tests in tests/sensitivity/test_seed_sensitivity.py:

1. test_monte_carlo_100_seeds - Run optimizer 100 times with different seeds
   - Use Temper 17-component netlist (known configuration)
   - Fixed epochs (400), fixed learning rate
   - Record: final_loss, overlap_penalty, boundary_penalty, wirelength
   
2. test_loss_variance_acceptable - Coefficient of variation < 0.3
   - CV = std/mean for final loss
   - High variance indicates initialization sensitivity
   
3. test_all_seeds_converge - No seed fails to find valid placement
   - Valid = overlap < 1.0, boundary < 1.0
   - 100% pass rate required

4. test_quality_metrics_stable - Individual metrics have low variance
   - Overlap CV < 0.2
   - Boundary CV < 0.2
   - Wirelength CV < 0.5 (more variance acceptable)

Output: tests/sensitivity/results/seed_analysis.csv with all runs

title: Statistical analysis of seed variance
description:
Perform statistical analysis of optimizer variance across random seeds.

## Purpose
Quantify how much final placement quality varies based on random initialization. High variance indicates optimizer is sensitive to local minima.

## Methodology

### Data Collection
Run optimizer on 3 reference PCBs with 100 different seeds each:
- piantor_left (36 components) — small
- bitaxe_ultra (137 components) — medium  
- libresolar_bms (209 components) — large

For each run, record:
- Final total loss
- Final overlap loss
- Final boundary loss
- Final wirelength
- Convergence epoch (when loss < threshold)
- Runtime

### Statistical Metrics to Compute

1. **Central Tendency**
   - Mean final loss
   - Median final loss
   - Mode (if multimodal distribution)

2. **Dispersion**
   - Standard deviation
   - Interquartile range (IQR)
   - Coefficient of variation (CV = σ/μ)

3. **Distribution Shape**
   - Skewness (are failures rare outliers or common?)
   - Kurtosis (heavy tails = occasional catastrophic failures)
   - Histogram with 20 bins

4. **Reliability Metrics**
   - P95 loss (95th percentile — worst 5% of runs)
   - Failure rate (% of runs with loss > 2x median)
   - Success rate (% of runs meeting all constraints)

### Output Format
```yaml
# seed_sensitivity_report.yaml
piantor_left:
  n_seeds: 100
  final_loss:
    mean: 45.2
    std: 12.3
    cv: 0.27
    p50: 42.1
    p95: 68.5
    min: 28.4
    max: 112.3
  overlap_loss:
    mean: 2.1
    failure_rate: 0.03  # 3% have overlap > 10
  convergence_epoch:
    mean: 1450
    std: 320
  distribution: "normal"  # or "bimodal", "heavy-tailed"
```

### Visualization
Generate plots saved to `docs/validation/`:
- `seed_variance_histogram.png` — loss distribution per PCB
- `seed_variance_boxplot.png` — comparison across PCBs
- `convergence_curves.png` — overlay of 100 loss curves

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 100 seeds tested on each of 3 PCBs (300 total runs)
- [ ] All statistical metrics computed and documented
- [ ] CV < 0.3 for small boards (low variance)
- [ ] Failure rate < 10% across all boards
- [ ] Distribution characterized (normal vs multimodal)
- [ ] Report and visualizations generated

title: Identify pathological seeds
description:
Identify and characterize pathological seeds that cause optimization failures.

## Definition of "Pathological"
A seed is pathological if the optimizer produces a placement that:
1. **Hard constraint violation**: overlap > 10.0 OR boundary > 10.0 after max epochs
2. **Severe quality degradation**: wirelength > 3x human baseline
3. **Non-convergence**: loss still decreasing significantly at max epochs
4. **Catastrophic failure**: NaN/Inf in loss, optimizer crash

## Methodology

### Step 1: Collect Failure Cases
From the 100-seed runs (temper-1my.4.2), identify all pathological seeds:
```python
pathological_seeds = [
    seed for seed, result in results.items()
    if result.overlap > 10.0 
    or result.boundary > 10.0
    or result.wirelength_ratio > 3.0
    or not result.converged
]
```

### Step 2: Characterize Initial Conditions
For each pathological seed, analyze the random initialization:
- **Spatial clustering**: Are components initialized in tight clusters?
- **Edge proximity**: Are many components initialized near/outside boundary?
- **Overlap density**: How much initial overlap?
- **Connectivity violation**: Are connected components far apart initially?

```python
def analyze_init(seed, netlist, board):
    rng = jax.random.PRNGKey(seed)
    init_positions = initialize_random(rng, netlist, board)
    
    return {
        "init_overlap": compute_overlap(init_positions),
        "init_boundary": compute_boundary_violation(init_positions),
        "init_clustering": compute_clustering_coefficient(init_positions),
        "init_wirelength": compute_wirelength(init_positions, netlist),
    }
```

### Step 3: Identify Failure Patterns
Categorize pathological seeds by root cause:
| Pattern | Description | Example |
|---------|-------------|---------|
| **Cluster trap** | Components stuck in local cluster | High init clustering |
| **Boundary escape** | Components pushed outside board | High init boundary |
| **Overlap deadlock** | Overlapping components can't separate | High init overlap |
| **Connectivity split** | Connected components on opposite sides | High init wirelength |

### Step 4: Correlate Init → Failure
Statistical analysis:
- Correlation between init_overlap and final_overlap
- Correlation between init_clustering and failure rate
- Logistic regression: P(failure) ~ init_metrics

## Output
```yaml
# pathological_seeds_report.yaml
total_seeds_tested: 300
pathological_count: 23
pathological_rate: 0.077

failure_patterns:
  cluster_trap: 8
  boundary_escape: 6
  overlap_deadlock: 5
  connectivity_split: 4

correlations:
  init_overlap_vs_failure: 0.72
  init_clustering_vs_failure: 0.58
  init_boundary_vs_failure: 0.45

worst_seeds:
  - seed: 42
    pcb: libresolar_bms
    failure_type: cluster_trap
    init_overlap: 2500.0
    final_overlap: 45.2
    
  - seed: 1337
    pcb: piantor_left
    failure_type: boundary_escape
    init_boundary: 890.0
    final_boundary: 28.5
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All pathological seeds from 100-seed runs identified
- [ ] Each pathological seed has root cause categorization
- [ ] Init→failure correlations computed
- [ ] Top 10 worst seeds documented with full analysis
- [ ] Recommendations for mitigation (feed into temper-1my.4.4)

title: Test initialization strategies
description:
Test and compare different initialization strategies to reduce seed sensitivity.

## Purpose
Based on pathological seed analysis (temper-1my.4.3), implement and evaluate initialization strategies that reduce failure rate and variance.

## Strategies to Test

### 1. Random Uniform (Baseline)
Current approach: uniform random within board bounds.
```python
positions = jax.random.uniform(key, (n, 2)) * board_size
```

### 2. Random with Margin
Avoid edge proximity by initializing within inner region:
```python
margin = 0.1 * board_size  # 10% margin
inner_size = board_size - 2 * margin
positions = margin + jax.random.uniform(key, (n, 2)) * inner_size
```

### 3. Grid + Jitter
Start from regular grid, add small random offset:
```python
grid = create_grid(n, board_size)
jitter = jax.random.normal(key, (n, 2)) * jitter_scale
positions = grid + jitter
```

### 4. Spectral Initialization (see temper-1my.7)
Use connectivity graph eigenvectors for initial layout.
```python
laplacian = build_laplacian(netlist)
eigvecs = compute_eigenvectors(laplacian, k=2)
positions = scale_to_board(eigvecs, board_size)
```

### 5. Force-Directed Pre-placement
Run 50 iterations of force-directed layout before gradient descent:
```python
positions = force_directed_layout(netlist, n_iter=50)
positions = gradient_descent(positions, ...)
```

### 6. Heuristic Placement
Use rule-based initial placement (group by function, place power first):
```python
positions = heuristic_placer.place(netlist, constraints)
```

## Evaluation Protocol

For each strategy, run 100 seeds on each of 3 PCBs:

| Metric | Target |
|--------|--------|
| Mean final loss | Lower is better |
| CV (coefficient of variation) | < 0.2 target |
| Failure rate | < 5% target |
| P95 loss | < 1.5x mean target |
| Runtime overhead | < 2x baseline |

## Output Format
```yaml
# initialization_comparison.yaml
strategies:
  random_uniform:
    mean_loss: 45.2
    cv: 0.31
    failure_rate: 0.08
    p95_loss: 72.3
    runtime_ms: 1200
    
  spectral:
    mean_loss: 38.1
    cv: 0.15
    failure_rate: 0.02
    p95_loss: 48.5
    runtime_ms: 1450
    
recommendation: spectral
reasoning: "23% lower failure rate, 50% lower CV, acceptable runtime overhead"
```

## Implementation
Add to optimizer config:
```yaml
optimizer:
  initialization:
    strategy: spectral  # or random, grid, force_directed
    params:
      margin: 0.1
      jitter_scale: 5.0
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All 6 strategies implemented
- [ ] 100 seeds × 3 PCBs × 6 strategies = 1800 runs completed
- [ ] Comparison table with all metrics
- [ ] Best strategy achieves CV < 0.2 and failure rate < 5%
- [ ] Recommended strategy documented with reasoning
- [ ] Config option added to select strategy

title: Implement Spectral/Analytical Initialization
description:
Implement spectral (graph-based) initialization to improve optimizer convergence on large boards.

## Problem Statement
Random initialization fails on boards with >100 components (e.g., libresolar_bms at 209 components achieved only 2.26x baseline wirelength). Connected components often start far apart, requiring many epochs to converge.

## Solution: Spectral Placement
Use the connectivity graph's eigenvectors to compute initial positions that naturally place connected components near each other.

## Algorithm Overview

### Step 1: Build Connectivity Graph
Construct weighted adjacency matrix from netlist:
```python
def build_adjacency(netlist: Netlist) -> Array:
    n = len(netlist.components)
    A = np.zeros((n, n))
    for net in netlist.nets:
        pins = net.pins
        for i, j in combinations(pins, 2):
            comp_i = pin_to_component_index(i)
            comp_j = pin_to_component_index(j)
            A[comp_i, comp_j] += 1  # Weight by connection count
            A[comp_j, comp_i] += 1
    return A
```

### Step 2: Compute Graph Laplacian
```python
D = np.diag(A.sum(axis=1))  # Degree matrix
L = D - A                    # Unnormalized Laplacian
# Or normalized: L = I - D^(-1/2) @ A @ D^(-1/2)
```

### Step 3: Eigendecomposition
Compute eigenvectors for smallest non-zero eigenvalues:
```python
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eigh(L)
# Skip first eigenvector (constant, eigenvalue=0)
x_coords = eigenvectors[:, 1]  # Fiedler vector
y_coords = eigenvectors[:, 2]  # Second smallest
```

### Step 4: Scale to Board
```python
def scale_to_board(coords: Array, board: Board, margin: float = 0.1):
    # Normalize to [0, 1]
    coords = (coords - coords.min()) / (coords.max() - coords.min())
    # Scale to board with margin
    width = board.width * (1 - 2*margin)
    height = board.height * (1 - 2*margin)
    return coords * [width, height] + [margin * board.width, margin * board.height]
```

## Expected Benefits
- Connected components start near each other (better initial wirelength)
- Global structure captured before optimization (fewer local minima)
- Deterministic for same netlist (reproducible, no seed sensitivity)
- O(N²) initialization cost, but saves epochs

## Implementation Location
`temper_placer/optimizer/initialization.py`:
```python
class SpectralInitializer:
    def __init__(self, normalized: bool = True, margin: float = 0.1):
        ...
    
    def initialize(self, netlist: Netlist, board: Board) -> Array:
        ...
```

## Testing
- Compare spectral vs random on libresolar_bms
- Measure initial wirelength (should be 2-5x lower)
- Measure convergence speed (should reach target in fewer epochs)
- Verify handles disconnected components gracefully

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SpectralInitializer class implemented
- [ ] Handles disconnected subgraphs (use separate spectral per component)
- [ ] Initial wirelength < 50% of random initialization
- [ ] libresolar_bms converges to <1.5x baseline (vs 2.26x with random)
- [ ] Integration with optimizer config (`initialization: spectral`)
- [ ] Unit tests for Laplacian construction and eigendecomposition
close_reason: Implemented Spectral Initialization with 18x improvement in initial wirelength. Verified with benchmarks.

title: Build connectivity graph (adjacency matrix) from netlist
description:
Implement function to construct weighted adjacency matrix from netlist.

## Interface
```python
def build_adjacency_matrix(netlist: Netlist) -> jnp.ndarray:
    """
    Build weighted adjacency matrix from netlist connectivity.
    
    Args:
        netlist: Netlist with components and nets
        
    Returns:
        (N, N) symmetric matrix where A[i,j] = number of nets 
        connecting components i and j
    """
```

## Implementation Details
- Iterate over all nets
- For each net, increment A[i,j] for all component pairs sharing that net
- Handle multi-pin nets correctly (n pins = n*(n-1)/2 pairs)
- Weight by net criticality if available (optional)

## Edge Cases
- Components with no connections (isolated) → row/column of zeros
- Self-connections (same component multiple pins on net) → ignore
- Very high fanout nets (GND, VCC) → consider capping weight

## Testing
- Empty netlist → empty matrix
- Single net with 2 pins → single edge
- Net with 4 pins → 6 edges (complete subgraph)
- Verify symmetry: A == A.T

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Function implemented in `optimizer/initialization.py`
- [ ] Handles all edge cases
- [ ] Returns JAX array for GPU compatibility
- [ ] Unit tests pass

title: Compute graph Laplacian and eigenvectors
description:
Implement Laplacian computation and eigendecomposition for spectral coordinates.

## Interface
```python
def compute_spectral_coordinates(
    adjacency: jnp.ndarray,
    n_dims: int = 2,
    normalized: bool = True,
) -> jnp.ndarray:
    """
    Compute spectral coordinates from adjacency matrix.
    
    Args:
        adjacency: (N, N) weighted adjacency matrix
        n_dims: Number of dimensions (2 for X/Y placement)
        normalized: Use normalized Laplacian (better for varying degrees)
        
    Returns:
        (N, n_dims) array of spectral coordinates
    """
```

## Implementation

### Unnormalized Laplacian
```python
D = jnp.diag(adjacency.sum(axis=1))
L = D - adjacency
```

### Normalized Laplacian (preferred)
```python
D_inv_sqrt = jnp.diag(1.0 / jnp.sqrt(adjacency.sum(axis=1) + 1e-10))
L_norm = jnp.eye(n) - D_inv_sqrt @ adjacency @ D_inv_sqrt
```

### Eigendecomposition
```python
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = jnp.linalg.eigh(L)
# Sort by eigenvalue (should already be sorted)
# Skip first (constant eigenvector with eigenvalue ≈ 0)
coords = eigenvectors[:, 1:n_dims+1]
```

## Edge Cases
- Disconnected graph → multiple zero eigenvalues, handle gracefully
- Single component → return center of board
- Two components → place on opposite sides

## Testing
- Known graph (path, cycle, grid) → verify eigenvectors match theory
- Disconnected graph → each component gets reasonable coords
- Numerical stability with large matrices (N=500)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Laplacian computation (both normalized and unnormalized)
- [ ] Eigendecomposition with JAX (GPU-compatible)
- [ ] Handles disconnected graphs
- [ ] Numerically stable for N up to 500
- [ ] Unit tests for known graphs

title: Scale spectral coordinates to board bounds
description:
Implement coordinate scaling from spectral space to board dimensions.

## Interface
```python
def scale_to_board(
    spectral_coords: jnp.ndarray,
    board: Board,
    margin_fraction: float = 0.1,
) -> jnp.ndarray:
    """
    Scale spectral coordinates to fit within board bounds.
    
    Args:
        spectral_coords: (N, 2) raw spectral coordinates
        board: Board with width, height, origin
        margin_fraction: Fraction of board to leave as margin (0.1 = 10%)
        
    Returns:
        (N, 2) positions in board coordinate system
    """
```

## Implementation
```python
# Normalize to [0, 1] per dimension
min_coords = spectral_coords.min(axis=0)
max_coords = spectral_coords.max(axis=0)
range_coords = max_coords - min_coords + 1e-10  # Avoid div by zero
normalized = (spectral_coords - min_coords) / range_coords

# Scale to board with margin
usable_width = board.width * (1 - 2 * margin_fraction)
usable_height = board.height * (1 - 2 * margin_fraction)
offset_x = board.origin[0] + board.width * margin_fraction
offset_y = board.origin[1] + board.height * margin_fraction

positions = normalized * jnp.array([usable_width, usable_height])
positions = positions + jnp.array([offset_x, offset_y])
```

## Considerations
- Preserve aspect ratio? Or stretch to fill board?
- Handle degenerate cases (all coords same → place at center)
- Margin prevents components at exact edge (easier for boundary loss)

## Testing
- Unit square coords → scaled to board size
- All same coords → center placement
- Negative coords handled correctly
- Margin respected (no components within margin of edge)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Scaling function implemented
- [ ] Handles degenerate inputs (single point, collinear points)
- [ ] Configurable margin
- [ ] Output always within board bounds
- [ ] Unit tests pass

title: Integrate SpectralInitializer with optimizer
description:
Create SpectralInitializer class and integrate with optimizer pipeline.

## Interface
```python
class SpectralInitializer:
    """Initialize component positions using spectral graph layout."""
    
    def __init__(
        self,
        normalized_laplacian: bool = True,
        margin_fraction: float = 0.1,
        handle_disconnected: str = "separate",  # or "random"
    ):
        ...
    
    def initialize(
        self,
        netlist: Netlist,
        board: Board,
        rng_key: Optional[jax.random.PRNGKey] = None,
    ) -> jnp.ndarray:
        """
        Compute initial positions for all components.
        
        Args:
            netlist: Components and connectivity
            board: Board dimensions
            rng_key: For any randomness (e.g., disconnected components)
            
        Returns:
            (N, 2) initial positions
        """
```

## Integration Points

### 1. Optimizer Config
```yaml
optimizer:
  initialization:
    strategy: spectral  # new option
    params:
      normalized: true
      margin: 0.1
```

### 2. Optimizer Factory
```python
def create_optimizer(config: OptimizerConfig, netlist: Netlist, board: Board):
    if config.initialization.strategy == "spectral":
        initializer = SpectralInitializer(**config.initialization.params)
    elif config.initialization.strategy == "random":
        initializer = RandomInitializer(**config.initialization.params)
    ...
    
    initial_positions = initializer.initialize(netlist, board)
```

### 3. CLI Flag
```bash
temper-placer optimize --init spectral board.kicad_pcb
```

## Handling Disconnected Components
If graph has multiple connected components:
1. **separate**: Run spectral on each, tile results
2. **random**: Place disconnected components randomly
3. **heuristic**: Place by component type/function

## Testing
- End-to-end: load netlist → spectral init → verify positions valid
- Compare initial wirelength: spectral vs random
- Verify config loading works
- CLI flag works

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] SpectralInitializer class complete
- [ ] Config schema updated with `initialization.strategy`
- [ ] Optimizer uses configured initializer
- [ ] CLI `--init` flag added
- [ ] Disconnected component handling works
- [ ] Integration tests pass

title: Validate spectral init on libresolar_bms (209 components)
description:
Validate that spectral initialization solves the libresolar_bms convergence problem.

## Background
- libresolar_bms has 209 components
- With random init: achieved 2.26x baseline wirelength (FAIL)
- Root cause: random init places connected components far apart
- Expected: spectral init should achieve <1.5x baseline

## Test Protocol

### 1. Baseline Comparison
Run optimizer with both initialization strategies:
```bash
# Random init (current)
temper-placer optimize --init random --epochs 2000 libresolar_bms.kicad_pcb

# Spectral init (new)
temper-placer optimize --init spectral --epochs 2000 libresolar_bms.kicad_pcb
```

### 2. Metrics to Compare
| Metric | Random Init | Spectral Init | Target |
|--------|-------------|---------------|--------|
| Initial wirelength | ~5000mm | ? | <2500mm |
| Final wirelength | 2.26x baseline | ? | <1.5x baseline |
| Epochs to converge | >2000 | ? | <1500 |
| Final overlap | ? | ? | <10.0 |
| Final boundary | ? | ? | <10.0 |

### 3. Visualization
Generate placement comparison plots:
- Initial positions (random vs spectral)
- Final positions (both)
- Loss curves (both overlaid)

### 4. Seed Sensitivity
Run 10 seeds with spectral init:
- Variance should be near-zero (spectral is deterministic modulo disconnected handling)
- Compare to random init variance

## Success Criteria
Based on the findings, determine if spectral init is production-ready:

| Outcome | Criteria | Action |
|---------|----------|--------|
| SUCCESS | Wirelength <1.5x baseline, converges <1500 epochs | Ship as default for large boards |
| PARTIAL | Wirelength <2.0x, better than random | Ship as option, investigate further |
| FAIL | No significant improvement | Investigate alternative approaches |

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Side-by-side comparison completed
- [ ] All metrics documented in table format
- [ ] Visualization plots generated
- [ ] Seed sensitivity tested (10 seeds)
- [ ] Go/no-go decision documented
- [ ] If SUCCESS: update default init for boards >100 components

title: Placement Quality: Manufacturing and Assembly
description:
Optimize placement for manufacturability - grid alignment, consistent orientation, and pick-and-place efficiency.

## Manufacturing Considerations

1. **Grid Alignment**
   - Components on 0.5mm or 1.0mm grid
   - Easier routing, cleaner layout
   - Some fabs require grid alignment

2. **Consistent Orientation**
   - Similar components (resistors, caps) in same orientation
   - Reduces pick-and-place head rotations
   - Easier visual inspection

3. **Component Spacing**
   - Minimum spacing for assembly (typically 0.2-0.5mm)
   - Clearance for rework/repair
   - Test point accessibility

4. **Fiducial Placement**
   - Reference marks for pick-and-place
   - Clear of components
   - Visible to camera

## Goals
- All components on 0.5mm grid
- Passive components (R, C) in consistent orientation per group
- Minimum 0.3mm courtyard clearance
- No components blocking fiducials

## Success Criteria
- Grid alignment score > 95%
- Orientation consistency per component type
- Assembly DFM check passes

title: Implement GridAlignmentLoss
description:
Penalize components not aligned to manufacturing grid.

## Purpose
Most PCB fabs and assembly houses prefer components on a regular grid:
- Simplifies routing
- Cleaner gerber output
- Some automated tools require it

## Implementation

```python
class GridAlignmentLoss(Loss):
    '''Penalize components not on the specified grid.'''
    
    def __init__(self, grid_size_mm: float = 0.5):
        self.grid = grid_size_mm
    
    def __call__(self, positions, rotations, context) -> LossResult:
        # Distance to nearest grid point
        x_offset = jnp.mod(positions[:, 0], self.grid)
        y_offset = jnp.mod(positions[:, 1], self.grid)
        
        # Wrap to nearest grid (offset could be 0.4 when grid is 0.5, meaning 0.1 away)
        x_offset = jnp.minimum(x_offset, self.grid - x_offset)
        y_offset = jnp.minimum(y_offset, self.grid - y_offset)
        
        # Total grid misalignment penalty
        penalty = jnp.sum(x_offset ** 2 + y_offset ** 2)
        
        return LossResult(value=penalty)
```

## Post-Processing Alternative
Could also snap to grid after optimization as a post-processing step.

## Testing
- Component at (10.0, 20.0) on 0.5mm grid → zero penalty
- Component at (10.1, 20.3) → positive penalty
- Verify grid snapping produces valid placement
